sections.directions: Directions: Each question in this section is based on the reasoning presented in a brief passage. In answering the questions, you should not make assumptions that are by commonsense standards implausible, superfluous, or incompatible with the passage. For some questions, more than one of the choices could conceivably answer the question. However, you are to choose the best answer; that is, choose the response that most accurately and completely answers the question.

items.stimulusText: Optical telescopes can be stationed either on isolated land, ensuring that city lights do not interfere with observations, or in outer space, orbiting Earth. However, because of advances in telescope technology, land telescopes are now superior in every respect. For example, many land telescopes now have exceptionally large mirrors that enable them to detect fainter objects than any space telescope can detect.
items.stemText: Each of the following, if true, would undermine the position taken above except:

options.optionContent: Unlike space telescopes, land telescopes can be operated only in clear weather.
The overall cost of doing research with land telescopes is greater than that of doing the same work with space telescopes.
Space telescopes are not affected by violent geological events such as earthquakes, whereas land telescopes are.
Space telescopes are more vulnerable to distortion from temperature extremes than are land telescopes.
Unlike space telescopes, land telescopes are subject to the corrosive effects of the atmosphere.

items.stimulusText: Philosopher: Any action that is fair never injures anyone. An action does not injure anyone only if that action is just, and thus every action that is fair must also be just. It follows that, because some merciful actions are just, some merciful actions are fair.
items.stemText: The reasoning in the philosopher's argument is flawed because the argument

options.optionContent: confuses what is required for fairness with what is sufficient for fairness
ignores the possibility that there may be an action that is fair and injures no one but is unjust
takes for granted that some actions that injure some parties are unfair to those parties
fails to distinguish between actions that are fair to all parties and actions that are just to all parties
fails to show the relationship between merciful actions and actions that do not injure anyone

items.stimulusText: Political philosopher: Some people advocate the general-happiness principle, according to which a government policy is justified if and only if it increases the overall happiness of those affected by it. But since any policy has infinitely many consequences, only a few of them predictable, an accurate estimate of a policy's total impact on happiness cannot be made. Accordingly, any reasonable justification of a government policy should appeal to something other than the general-happiness principle.
items.stemText: Which one of the following principles, if valid, most supports the political philosopher's reasoning?

options.optionContent: The welfare of people should not in every case be identified with their happiness.
Principles impractical in their application ought not to be employed.
Governmental policies should not be justified in the same way as the actions of individuals.
Political principles are acceptable if at least some of the policies they justify also commend themselves to common sense.
Any government policy based on unreliable predictions is unlikely to be the most efficient means of attaining the government's objectives.

items.stimulusText: Francesca: It is clear where the convention should be held: Rockland has a special tradition of support for our association and only Rockland has offered us significant discounts, not to mention the unusual beauty of the Rockland hills in winter. It is true that our newsletter prefers Langhorne to Rockland. But that publication is under the sway of the coterie that controls our association's top leadership.
items.stemText: Which one of the following most accurately expresses the main conclusion of Francesca's argument?

options.optionContent: The coverage of the convention debate by the association's newsletter has been biased.
Rockland has a tradition of support and funding for the association.
The association's top leadership is unduly influenced by a small group of powerful people.
The association should hold its next convention in Rockland.
The powerful group that has taken control of the association's top leadership should be resisted.

items.stimulusText: Some argue that because the same product standards will be applied throughout Europe as a result of European economic unification, European goods will become more homogenous. But since unification will enrich the economies of the participating countries, people will have greater incomes and demand a greater variety of goods than they currently do. Therefore, strengthening European economic unity will actually increase the diversity of products made in Europe.
items.stemText: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

options.optionContent: New economic opportunities will allow some European companies to produce goods that previously had to be imported.
Unified standards for European products will allow companies to streamline production of some goods, so the companies will be able to produce these goods more efficiently.
As trade barriers between European countries weaken, the volume of European goods traded within Europe will increase.
Increased accessibility to diverse consumers will lead to new opportunities for many European companies to market their stockpiled goods.
Weakened national barriers will enable small companies to combine their resources and increase the prices of the goods they produce.

items.stimulusText: A region wherein all telephone numbers now have the same area code is being split into two regions, each with its own area code. Since the residential section of the region has more telephone numbers than does the section containing mostly businesses, the residential section should keep the old area code and the business section should get the new area code. After all, this will result in fewer incidents of dialing the wrong number than the reverse distribution of area codes.
items.stemText: The reasoning in the argument is flawed because the argument

options.optionContent: takes for granted that the current single area code cannot provide enough numbers for over half of the whole region
improperly relies on a vague distinction between residences and businesses
takes for granted that there will be no future growth in either section of the region
overlooks the possibility that businesses typically receive more calls than do residences
overlooks the possibility of adding more than one new area code to the region

items.stimulusText: Many historians theorize that the achievements of some seventeenth-century scientists not only paved the way for the technological advances of following centuries but also signaled a profound transformation of thought about nature. The historians hold that whereas earlier thinkers had considered nature an organic being, these scientists began to think of it in purely mechanical terms. But the belief that there was a radical transformation of this sort did not arise until the twentieth century. Thus, the historians' theory is false.
items.stemText: The conclusion of the argument follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?

options.optionContent: Radical transformations in scientific thinking cannot be recognized as such except in the light of commentary by later generations.
No widespread belief that a radical transformation in thought has occurred can arise if no such transformation has actually occurred.
No one can participate in a radical transformation of thought about a subject without believing that such a transformation has occurred.
The technological advances that followed from seventeenth-century scientific work did not require that nature be thought of in purely mechanical terms.
The notion that collective human thought can undergo radical transformations is a product of twentieth-century thinking and was previously unknown.

items.stimulusText: Manager: Bureaucratization is a problem only when it is not strict and complete. Bureaucracy—a hierarchy of offices each of which is devoted to a specialized task—is the most efficient type of social organization yet devised. Thus, businesses should implement bureaucracy more strictly.
items.stemText: The definition of bureaucracy given figures in the manager's argument in which one of the following ways?

options.optionContent: It acts as a premise that guarantees the truth of the conclusion.
It provides background information but is not necessary for drawing the conclusion.
It serves as a rhetorical move designed to sway emotions.
It has the effect of contradicting the argument's conclusion.
It is designed to contradict an opposing claim and show it to be false.

items.stimulusText: The custodian maintains that if she had stolen the formula and sold it to our company's competitors, she would have collected a fortune and quit her job. However, she is still working at our company for minimum wage. Thus she must not have stolen the formula.
items.stemText: The flawed pattern of reasoning in which one of the following is most similar to the flawed pattern of reasoning in the argument above?

options.optionContent: The pet owner maintains that if the raccoon had rabies and bit his dog, then the dog would have fallen ill. But the dog didn't fall ill after being bitten by the raccoon. Therefore the raccoon doesn't have rabies.

The presiding election official states that if she had accepted a bribe from the incumbent's party and falsified voting results, the incumbent would have won the election. The incumbent was soundly defeated, however, so the election official must not have accepted a bribe from the incumbent's party.

The airplane manufacturer claims that if there were a design flaw in its newest passenger jet, the flaw would have been discovered during testing. Since no problems were discovered during testing, there must be no design flaws in the manufacturer's airplanes.

The promoter of the Snow-Dog Jamboree said that it would be successful if there were good weather and ample parking. Even though the weather was good, the Jamboree did not do well. Thus there must not have been ample parking.

The government official argues that she was not the one who leaked the sensitive information to the press, because she had no motive for doing so, and whoever leaked the information would have had the motive and the opportunity to do so.

items.stimulusText: Mahogany seedlings can establish themselves only immediately after natural disturbances such as severe floods. The seedlings grow only where such sudden disturbances have cleared away all competing vegetation. For this reason, all the mahogany trees in any single area are roughly the same age and size. As a result, mahogany loggers generally cut entire older stands of the trees, in which every tree is of marketable size.
items.stemText: Which one of the following is most strongly supported by the information above?

options.optionContent: Natural disturbances, such as severe floods, do not typically clear away entire stands of older mahogany trees.
Mahogany seedlings generally cannot establish themselves within stands of large, mature mahogany trees.
When mahogany loggers cut entire older stands of the trees, they do not generally clear away all the vegetation that could compete with new mahogany seedlings.

If a natural disturbance, such as a severe flood, has cleared away all the vegetation that could compete with mahogany seedlings in a given area, then at least some mahogany seedlings will establish themselves in that area.

Once mahogany seedlings have established themselves in a given area, natural disturbances such as severe floods do not typically affect the mahogany stand in that area until the seedlings grow into trees.

items.stimulusText: Kathy, who has worked for Acme Corporation for five years, will resign if she does not receive what she considers to be a meaningful promotion this year. Acme management has decided to promote Kathy to a more prestigious position but with no increase in pay. On this basis we can conclude that Kathy will resign.

options.optionContent: Kathy does not consider the promotion meaningful in that it carries no pay increase
Acme could afford to give Kathy a substantial increase in pay without significantly reducing the corporation's profits
Kathy considers the change to a more prestigious position to be of absolutely no value
most employees who have worked at Acme for at least five years have received what they consider to be meaningful promotions
if Acme decided simply to give Kathy a pay raise this year, she would not resign

items.stimulusText: Mercury contamination of lakes and oceans is responsible for the increasing number of cases of mercury poisoning of wildlife. The mercury found in lakes and oceans is traceable almost entirely to mercury in industrial waste. However, even if all dumping of mercury-contaminated industrial waste were eliminated today, the quantity of mercury in lakes and oceans would continue to increase for the foreseeable future.
items.stemText: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy in the information above?

options.optionContent: Industrial production of mercury through the burning of oil and coal has been on the decline for the past decade.
The average amount of mercury that is released into the upper atmosphere during volcanic eruptions is relatively constant from year to year.
The mercury in lakes and oceans is primarily due to the gradual erosion of soil that had been contaminated with mercury from industrial waste.
Some fish that have been contaminated with mercury suffer no ill effects even though that contamination makes them poisonous to human beings.
Pressure by environmentalists has not yet forced industry to introduce safer methods for disposing of mercury waste.

description: The Drill Set contains logical reasoning question types. Each question involves analyzing a short passage and evaluating its reasoning, emphasizing critical thinking skills pivotal in legal reasoning.
